text: This is a robotic-assisted right lower lobectomy, and this patient had a cancer in the right lower lobe, which you can see visualize the dark spot on the lung.
I usually do my mediastinal lymph node dissection.
This starts with mobilizing the inferior pulmonary ligament.
I'll remove any station 8 or 9 lymph nodes, and I go all the way up to the inferior pulmonary vein, which is visualized.
I sometimes will go to the anterior mediastinum a little bit here to define where the middle of vein, you can see the middle of vein to the right coming off the superior vein.
So the inferior vein is going to be separate.
I then retract the lung anteriorly and I dissect the posterior mediastinum.
And in this location, you can get station eight lymph nodes along the esophagus, which is visualized here.
And then I go along the bronchus to get into the subcarinal area to get station seven lymph nodes.
I use the long bipolar grasper as my energy source, which has very little spread and cauterizes quite nicely.
This is the junction between the upper and lower lobe bronchus.
There's always a lymph node there.
The artery that you can see to the bottom of the screen is the posterior ascending artery.
And if you skeletonize this area, then you open up the fissure.
I then retract the lung inferiorly.
And I'll look in station two and four areas along the trachea.
And then I will go to the anterior mediastinum to finish dissecting out the vein.
In between the upper lobe and lower lobe veins, there's frequently a lymph node.
After I've done my mediastinal lymph node dissection, I then come into the fissure into the hilum.
And if you've done a good job dissecting the posterior mediastinum, then you can easily get through the fissure between the upper and lower lobes.
I have a vessel loop around it.
I'm going to come in now with the robotic green stapler.
This is the 45 stapler, and it slides through quite nicely, and I can divide the fissure between the upper lobe and the lower lobe.
I usually get the vessel loop out so it's not included in the staple line.
This is now divided, and once that's divided, it really opens up the fissure as visualized here.
That is the middle lobe artery.
to the top of the screen.
There's usually a lymph node between the upper and lower lobe, the middle and lower lobe arteries.
This is removed, and again, that will open up the basilar segmental arteries to the lower lobe.
This is the superior segmental artery.
Place a vessel loop around.
We bring the stapler in.
The anvil always comes up.
I rotate it down, clutch my hand,
get the right position for the stapler, and then I bring the stapler in, again removing the vessel loop, and I divide the artery.
Once this artery is divided, it again opens up the fissure.
Now I can divide the basilar segmental arteries, again passing a vessel loop.
I like vessel loops because it holds a vessel up away from other structures.
The stapler comes in, anvil up.
I rotate it down.
I then get the angle that I need.
I bring the stapler in.
I engage it, then I divide the artery.
So now the fissure has opened up quite nicely.
The fissure between the middle lobe and lower lobe is a little bit incomplete here.
So I want to get the fissure started with the 45 green stapler.
And once that's divided, I can get a vessel loop around to complete the fissure.
And again, I get a vessel loop to hold it up away from the arteries and the other structures.
I bring the green stapler in and I divide the bronchus.
Now they divide the fissure.
Now the artery and the fissures are divided.
We now have the inferior pulmonary veins.
I'll retract the lung superiorly.
And we can see the vein visualized.
Again, I get a vessel loop around it.
And I always use four arms with the robot.
You can see my third arm is holding up the lung.
I've got the vessel loop holding the inferior pulmonary vein away from other structures.
I can come in with a white stapler.
I fix my angle.
I engage it, and then I fire it.
And then once we have completed the arteries, veins, and fissure, we're now ready to divide the bronchus.
So I'll retract along again inferiorly.
I'll skeletonize the bronchus to make sure that I'm away from the middle lobe bronchus, which you can see to the right going to the middle lobe.
I'll bring the green stapler in.
In this case, I like the 30 stapler because it's got the anvil tip, which is nice and smooth.
I'm not going to graze or tear any vessels as I come in.
So I've engaged it.
I've stapled and now divided.
I'm still old school, so I inflate the lung just to make sure the middle lobe and upper lobe come up normally, which they do.
So now our lower lobectomy is completed.
I now will fill the chest full of saline.
I'll inflate the lung and look for any air leaks.
A third of our lobectomies go home the next day and we can't do it if they have an air leak.
And visualize in the middle lobe, there's a small air leak.
So we'll come in and we will seal that with Progel.
Alternatively, you could cauterize it or staple it.
We put the chest tube in a direct vision.
It's a 20 French chest tube.
We position it anteriorly.
And once we have the chest tube in place, we then bring our specimen bag in, which is a 15 anchor bag.
Or more recently, we've used the air seal, which is a 12 millimeter.
So we use a Medtronic 12 millimeter or 1200 milliliter bag.
This is the diaphragm.
Usually ends up being about a one or two inch rent in the direction of the fibers.
We use a 2-0 monofilament V-lock suture, which is six inches long.
And this is visualizing repair of the diaphragm.
We go through the loop.
The V-lock suture has little barbs on it, so you don't need to tie it, and this is the completed result.
As we have repaired the diaphragm, we'll hold that up, and we'll cut it, and then we will get the suture out, and then we inflate the lung under direct vision.
